title: Today's AI Brief: Claude's new model is more 'honest' when it messes up
summary: Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 model prioritizes honesty when making mistakes, while OpenAI's Codex helps build self-improving tax agents.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Claude’s new model is more ‘honest’ when it messes up
imageCaption: Claude’s new model is more ‘honest’ when it messes up (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've got a few interesting updates for you from the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Anthropic's Claude is getting a bit more honest when it messes up. Their new model, Opus 4.8, is launching on Thursday, and it's designed to be more transparent about its mistakes. I think this is a big deal because, let's face it, AI models aren't perfect, and it's how they handle mistakes that matters.

According to The Verge, Claude's new model is being touted as more 'honest' when it makes errors. This is a step towards more trustworthy AI systems, and I'm curious to see how this plays out in real-world applications. For instance, imagine using AI for critical tasks like healthcare or finance - having a model that can say 'I don't know' or 'I made a mistake' could be a game-changer.

Next, OpenAI is working with Thrive and Crete to build self-improving tax agents using Codex. This project aims to automate tax filings, improve accuracy, and speed up workflows. I've always found tax season to be a bit of a headache, so it's cool to see AI being used to simplify complex tasks and reduce the burden on humans.

Claude’s new model is more ‘honest’ when it messes up, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
